regimens.regimen_name: ADT with Docetaxel and Abiraterone (Triplet Therapy)

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: PEACE-1 was a 2×2 factorial design. Grade ≥3 AEs occurred in 63% of triplet vs 52% of ADT+docetaxel. Most common grade ≥3 AEs with triplet: neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, fatigue, neuropathy. Intended for high-volume mCSPC patients fit for chemotherapy [556].

regimens.regimen_name: ADT with Docetaxel and Darolutamide (Triplet Therapy)

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: ARASENS showed improved OS at 4 years: 62.7% vs 50.4%. Also improved time to CRPC (HR 0.36), skeletal event-free survival (HR 0.61), and time to subsequent therapy (HR 0.39). Adverse events of any grade and grade 3-5 were similar between arms. Rash (16.6% vs 13.5%) and hypertension (13.7% vs 9.2%) were more frequent in darolutamide arm [557,559].

regimens.regimen_name: ADT with Docetaxel and Enzalutamide (Triplet Therapy)

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: ENZAMET compared enzalutamide+ADT to first-generation antiandrogen+ADT with concurrent docetaxel allowed. OS improved regardless of docetaxel use. Most common grade ≥3 AEs: febrile neutropenia (6%), fatigue (6%), hypertension (10%) [546,547].

regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.schedule: Once daily (without docetaxel); Twice daily (with docetaxel)

regimens.notes: LATITUDE: 5-year OS 52.5% vs 34.5%. STAMPEDE: OS benefit in M1, N1, and M0 subgroups. Alternative fine-particle formulation at 500 mg with methylprednisolone 4 mg PO BID is category 2B. Low-dose abiraterone 250 mg/day with low-fat breakfast also an option for patients who cannot tolerate or afford standard dose [538,539,541,542].

regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: TITAN: 44-month median follow-up, OS not reached vs 52.2 months. More common AEs: rash, hypothyroidism, ischemic heart disease. HRQOL maintained during treatment [543,545].

regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: ENZAMET: OS benefit maintained at 68-month follow-up. ARCHES: OS benefit with 34% risk reduction, may be underestimated due to 32% crossover to enzalutamide. AEs include fatigue, seizures, hypertension [546-549].

regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: ARANOTE: rPFS improved but OS not yet significant. FDA approved June 2025. Not category 1 because OS benefit not demonstrated for doublet therapy. Consistent benefit across low- and high-volume subgroups [550].

regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: Most patients (78%) had high-volume and/or synchronous disease. Grade 3/4 AEs more common with niraparib arm (75% vs 59%). Most common grade 3/4 AEs: anemia (29.1% vs 4.6%), hypertension (26.5% vs 18.4%). Treatment-emergent AEs leading to death: 14 vs 7 participants. OS data immature. Can be used following docetaxel completion in triplet therapy or after EBRT to primary tumor. Benefit in low-volume disease is controversial [560].

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

regimens.notes: CHAARTED: Median OS 57.6 vs 47.2 months. Subgroup analysis showed OS benefit predominantly in high-volume disease (HR 0.63). Low-volume disease did not show definitive survival benefit (HR 1.04). STAMPEDE confirmed benefit. Panel notes triplets of ADT+docetaxel+ARPI show improved OS over ADT+docetaxel, so docetaxel alone is not included as preferred option but may be considered [551-553].

drugs.name: Leuprolide
drugs.schedule: Per standard ADT schedule (optional, with or without)

biomarkers.requirement: PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥2 ng/mL above nadir after RT or ≥1 ng/mL after RP ± post-op RT; not candidate for pelvic-directed therapy

regimens.notes: EMBARK enrolled patients with high-risk BCR: M0 by CT/MRI/bone scan, PSADT ≤9 months. Median follow-up 60.7 months. Common AEs: hot flashes, fatigue. Enzalutamide monotherapy associated with gynecomastia (45%), nipple pain (15%), breast tenderness (14%) [478].

regimens.setting: High-risk BCR2 (post-RP, received prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not candidate for RT)

biomarkers.requirement: PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥0.5 ng/mL; prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not candidate for RT

regimens.notes: PRESTO: Median PSA-PFS 24.9 months (ADT+apalutamide) vs 20.3 months (ADT alone). Planned treatment duration 52 weeks. Common AEs: hot flashes (78%), fatigue (55%), hypertension (31% any grade, 7-8% grade ≥3 in arms A and B) [479].

COU-AA-302: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC, no visceral metastases, no prior ketoconazole. PSA decline ≥50%: 62% vs 24%. Radiographic responses: 36% vs 16%. Fine-particle formulation 500 mg with methylprednisolone 4 mg BID also available [583,584].
PREVAIL: Chemotherapy-naive mCRPC. No food restrictions. Concurrent prednisone permitted but not required. Seizure risk 0.9% per FDA label [593,594].

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.schedule: Daily (may omit on chemo day if dexamethasone given)

regimens.notes: Every-3-week schedule is standard. Alternative: 50 mg/m2 every 2 weeks (phase 2 data showed similar OS with less toxicity). ≥8 cycles may be associated with better OS. Duration based on benefit and toxicities assessment [607-611].

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.schedule: Daily (may omit on chemo day if dexamethasone given)

regimens.notes: TROPIC: 25 mg/m2 OS benefit vs mitoxantrone. PROSELICA: 20 mg/m2 noninferior to 25 mg/m2 for OS with less toxicity. 20 mg/m2 is recommended for fit patients; 25 mg/m2 may be considered for healthy patients. Biweekly 16 mg/m2 with G-CSF an option for patients ≥65 years (CABASTY trial). Grade ≥3 AEs: febrile neutropenia, diarrhea, fatigue [613-620].

biomarkers.requirement: Visceral metastases, low PSA and bulky disease, high LDH, high CEA, lytic bone metastases, NEPC histology, or unfavorable genomics (defects in ≥2 of PTEN, TP53, RB1)

regimens.notes: Post-hoc analysis showed patients with aggressive variant disease had longer median PFS with combination (7.5 vs 1.7 months; P = .017). Most common grade 3-5 AEs: fatigue, anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia — all more common in the combination arm [622].

biomarkers.requirement: No definitive evidence of progression on prior docetaxel in castration-sensitive or mCRPC setting

regimens.notes: Retrospective data from GETUG-AFU 15 suggests docetaxel may benefit CRPC patients who received docetaxel in the CSPC setting without definitive progression. Not recommended for patients who received docetaxel in CSPC setting without prior ARPI and then present with pre-ARPI mCRPC [611,612].

regimens.setting: Post-ARPI (any prior docetaxel), BRCAm or HRRm

biomarkers.requirement: BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, BARD1, BRIP1, CDK12, CHEK1, CHEK2, FANCL, PALB2, RAD51B, RAD51C, RAD51D, or RAD54L mutation (germline and/or somatic)

regimens.notes: PROfound: Efficacy driven by BRCA2/BRCA1/ATM cohort. OS benefit maintained despite 66% crossover in control arm. Adverse events: anemia (including requiring transfusion), fatigue, nausea/vomiting, thrombocytopenia, creatinine elevation. Theoretical risk of myelodysplasia/AML [652,653].

biomarkers.requirement: Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation (germline and/or somatic)

regimens.notes: TRITON2: BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC post-ARPI and taxane. TRITON3: rPFS improved with rucaparib vs physician's choice. Should not be used in patients with HRR mutations other than BRCA1/2. Adverse events: anemia, fatigue, nausea [654-656].

biomarkers.requirement: Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation (germline and/or somatic)

regimens.notes: PROpel: rPFS 24.8 vs 16.6 months overall. Final OS not significant in full cohort (P = .054). Post-hoc OS benefit in BRCAm: HR 0.29 (95% CI, 0.14-0.56). No OS benefit in non-HRRm or non-BRCAm/other HRRm subgroups [661,662].

biomarkers.requirement: BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, ATR, CDK12, CHEK2, FANCA, MLH1, MRE11A, NBN, PALB2, or RAD51C mutation (germline and/or somatic)

regimens.notes: TALAPRO-2: HRRm cohort OS 45.1 vs 31.1 months. BRCAm had 77% lower risk of radiographic progression (HR 0.23). Grade 3-4 AEs: anemia (31%), thrombocytopenia (9%), neutropenia (8%). Dose interruption due to AEs in 75% of talazoparib group vs 23% placebo [664,665].

biomarkers.requirement: Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation (germline and/or somatic)

regimens.notes: MAGNITUDE: HRRm group rPFS 16.5 vs 13.7 months. Non-BRCA HRRm subgroup showed no benefit. Non-HRRm cohort: futility declared. Second interim analysis suggested possible OS benefit (HR 0.54; 95% CI, 0.33-0.90; P = .0181). Grade ≥3 AEs: anemia 28.3% vs 7.6%, hypertension 14.6% vs 12.3% [666,667].

regimens.setting: Pre-ARPI (after progression on one ARPI in M0 CRPC), BRCA1/2m
regimens.notes: TRITON3: Post-ARPI, no prior chemotherapy for mCRPC. Primary endpoint met. Should not be used in patients with HRR gene mutations other than BRCA1/2 [656].

regimens.regimen_name: Olaparib plus Abiraterone (Pre-ARPI mCRPC — without specific BRCA requirement)

biomarkers.marker: None specific (but benefit primarily in BRCAm/HRRm)
biomarkers.requirement: Untreated mCRPC (PROpel enrollment); benefit strongest in BRCAm

regimens.notes: FDA approved for BRCAm mCRPC. While PROpel enrolled ITT population without HRR requirement, the Panel includes this as category 1 for BRCAm mCRPC. Fine-particle abiraterone may be substituted for standard formulation [661,662].

Use of this combination for patients who have received prior ARPI without prior docetaxel is controversial (category 2B) because a benefit of this combination over use of a PARP inhibitor alone has not been shown in this setting [666,667].

biomarkers.requirement: BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, ATR, CDK12, CHEK2, FANCA, MLH1, MRE11A, NBN, PALB2, or RAD51C mutation

regimens.notes: Use of talazoparib/enzalutamide for patients who have received prior ARPI without prior docetaxel is controversial (category 2B) because a benefit of this combination over a PARP inhibitor alone has not been shown in this setting, but responses are likely [664,665].

regimens.setting: Any prior therapy (useful in certain circumstances)

biomarkers.requirement: Microsatellite instability-high or mismatch repair deficiency

regimens.notes: Prevalence of MMR deficiency in mCRPC is 2%-5%. If MSI-H/dMMR found, refer to genetic counseling for Lynch syndrome assessment. Limited data suggest benefit in TMB ≥10 mut/mB mCRPC (Lenis AT, et al. Clin Canc Res 2024;30:3894-3903). May cause severe immune-mediated adverse reactions [592,625-631].

regimens.setting: Any prior therapy (asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic)

drugs.name: Sipuleucel-T
drugs.schedule: Leukapheresis, then 3 infusions 2 weeks apart (at weeks 0, 2, 4)

biomarkers.requirement: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic; no liver metastases; ECOG 0-1; life expectancy >6 months

regimens.notes: 22% mortality risk reduction. 18.2% had prior chemotherapy. Not recommended for small cell prostate cancer/NEPC. Benefit has not been reported in patients with visceral metastases. Limited data to support efficacy post-chemotherapy. Common complications: chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%) [623,624].

regimens.setting: Post-ARPI, post-docetaxel or pre-docetaxel (delay taxane); PSMA-positive

biomarkers.requirement: ≥1 PSMA-positive lesion; no dominant PSMA-negative metastatic lesions (defined as bone with soft tissue ≥1.0 cm, lymph nodes ≥2.5 cm short axis, solid organ ≥1.0 cm)

regimens.notes: VISION: Post-ARPI and post-taxane setting. PSMAfore: Pre-taxane, post-ARPI. OS not significantly different in PSMAfore due to crossover (57% of control arm received Lu-177-PSMA-617). Grade ≥3 AEs (VISION): anemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, fatigue — more common with Lu-177-PSMA-617. PSMA imaging should be used to determine eligibility [668,669].

regimens.setting: Symptomatic bone metastases, no visceral metastases

biomarkers.requirement: Symptomatic bone metastases; no visceral metastatic disease

regimens.notes: 57% received prior docetaxel. Extends time to first SRE (median 15.6 vs 9.8 months). Grade 3/4 hematologic toxicity low: neutropenia 3%, thrombocytopenia 6%, anemia 13%. Should not be used in patients with visceral metastases. Concurrent use with enzalutamide may be considered (PEACE-3 trial). Concomitant denosumab or zoledronic acid recommended. Increased fracture risk when combined with ARPI without bone agent [670-676].

PEACE-3: rPFS 16.4 vs 19.4 months, OS 35.0 vs 42.3 months. Grade ≥3 AEs: 65.6% vs 55.8%. Fractures: 24.3% vs 13.4% (without bone agents fracture rate was 45.9% vs 22.3%). Mandatory concomitant bone-protecting agent use [675,676].

trial_data.SRE_delay: Median 20.7 vs 17.1 months (HR, 0.82; P = .008 for superiority)

regimens.notes: Preferred over zoledronic acid based on longer time to first SRE. Similar spinal cord compression, need for RT, and pathologic fracture rates. Hypocalcemia more common with denosumab (13% vs 6%). ONJ 1%-2%. Supplemental calcium and vitamin D recommended [684,687].

biomarkers.requirement: CRPC with bone metastases; creatinine clearance ≥30 mL/min

regimens.notes: Every-12-week dosing noninferior to every-4-week dosing (Himelstein et al. JAMA 2017). Dose reduction for CrCl 30-60 mL/min; hold for CrCl <30 mL/min. Not recommended in CSPC bone metastases [684,685,688,689].
Treatment for small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer is extrapolated from NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer. Refer to NCCN SCLC guidelines for additional options [679-683].
Extrapolated from NCCN SCLC guidelines. Carboplatin is an alternative to cisplatin [679-683].
Refer to NCCN SCLC guidelines [679-683].

biomarkers.requirement: Small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer or aggressive variant mCRPC

regimens.notes: Can be considered for aggressive variant mCRPC or NEPC [622].

drugs.name: Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT)
drugs.dose: Per MDT dose guidelines (e.g., 30-35 Gy in 5 fractions)

biomarkers.requirement: Oligometastatic disease (≤5 by CT/MRI/bone scan or ≤10 by PSMA-PET)

regimens.guideline_category: Preferred (metachronous oligorecurrent); Useful in certain circumstances (synchronous)
regimens.notes: ORIOLE/STOMP combined analysis: pooled HR 0.44 (P < .001) for PFS at 52.5-month follow-up. EXTEND: PFS improved with MDT vs intermittent ADT alone. ARTO (mCRPC): PFS improved with MDRT+abiraterone (HR 0.35; P < .001). MDT may also use lymph node dissection in select cases [565-570,677].

biomarkers.requirement: Symptomatic patients unable to tolerate other therapies

regimens.notes: No survival benefit demonstrated in two randomized trials. May provide palliation for pain. Adverse events similar to docetaxel but lower rates of grade 3-4 neutropenic fevers [633,634].

biomarkers.requirement: Elevated fracture risk based on FRAX; 10-year risk of hip fracture ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture ≥20%

regimens.notes: Recommended when absolute fracture risk warrants drug therapy. Baseline DEXA before ADT; follow-up DEXA after 1 year of therapy. Hypocalcemia risk factors: blastic bone metastases, renal impairment, vitamin D deficiency. Do not stop without bisphosphonate coverage due to rebound bone loss [528,532].
Alternative to denosumab and zoledronic acid for treatment-related bone loss during ADT [531].
Alternative to denosumab and alendronate for treatment-related bone loss during ADT. Monitor renal function [529,530].
